request.question: For Which team did the second man to win a championship in three different decades play ?

title: Eloise Jarvis McGraw
Mark Jonathan Harris
Eastern Shore League
Bobby Unser
Tim Duncan
Graeme Hick
John Salley
Gene Upshaw
Herb Thomas (baseball)
Duran Duran discography
sentences: Eloise Jarvis McGraw (December 9, 1915 – November 30, 2000) was an American author of children's books and young adult novels.
She was awarded the Newbery Honor three times in three different decades, for her novels "Moccasin Trail" (1952), "The Golden Goblet" (1962), and "The Moorchild" (1997). "
A Really Weird Summer" (1977) won an Edgar Award for Best Juvenile Mystery from the Mystery Writers of America.
McGraw had a very strong interest in history, and among the many books she wrote for children are "Greensleeves", "The Seventeenth Swap", and "Mara, Daughter of the Nile".
A Lewis Carroll Shelf Award was given to "Moccasin Trail" in 1963.
Mark Jonathan Harris (born 1941) is an American documentary filmmaker probably best known for his films "" (2000) and "The Long Way Home" (1997).
He has directed three documentaries which have gone on to win Oscars, across three different decades.
The Eastern Shore Baseball League was a class D minor league baseball league that operated on the Delmarva Peninsula for parts of three different decades.
The league's first season was in 1922 and the last was in 1949, although the years were not consecutive, and featured teams from Maryland, Delaware, and Virginia.
The first incarnation lasted from 1922 to mid-1928 (disbanded in July), the second from 1937–41, and the third from 1946–49.
Though the level of play was competitive and many future major leaguers gained experience in the ESBL, funding the league remained a constant problem for the rural franchises.
Robert William "Bobby" Unser (born February 20, 1934) is an American former automobile racer.
He is the brother of Al Unser, Jerry Unser and Louis Unser, the father of Robby Unser, and the uncle of Al Unser, Jr. and Johnny Unser.
He is one of ten drivers to win the Indianapolis 500 three or more times, and one of only two (followed by Rick Mears) to have won the 500 in three different decades (1968, 75, 81).
Bobby has also been a spokesman and advocate of many commercial products.

Timothy Theodore Duncan (born April 25, 1976) is an American retired professional basketball player who played his entire 19-year career with the San Antonio Spurs of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
Widely considered to be the greatest power forward of all time, he is a five-time NBA champion, two-time NBA MVP, three-time NBA Finals MVP, NBA All-Star Game MVP and NBA Rookie of the Year.
He is also a 15-time NBA All-Star and the only player to be selected to both the All-NBA and All-Defensive Teams in all of his first 13 seasons.
Graeme Ashley Hick MBE (born 23 May 1966) is a former English cricketer who played 65 Test matches and 120 One Day Internationals for England.

He played county cricket for Worcestershire for his entire English domestic career, a period of well over twenty years, and in 2008 he surpassed Graham Gooch's record for the most matches in all forms of the game combined.

He scored more than 40,000 first-class runs, mostly from number three in the order, and he is one of only three players to have passed 20,000 runs in List A cricket (Graham Gooch and Sachin Tendulkar are the others) and is one of only twenty-five players to have scored 100 centuries in first-class cricket.
He is the only cricketer who scored first-class triple hundreds in three different decades (1988, 1997 and 2002).
He is the second highest run scorer of all time after Graham Gooch.

Despite these achievements, he is commonly held to have underachieved in international cricket, a view based on comparison of Hick's overall first-class batting average of 52.23 "vis-à-vis" his Test average of 31.32.
John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964) is an American retired professional basketball player, actor and talk show host.

He was the first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises, as well as the first player (and only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan) in the NBA to win a championship in three different decades.

Eugene Thurman Upshaw Jr. (August 15, 1945 – August 20, 2008), also known as "Uptown Gene", was an American football player for the Oakland Raiders of the American Football League (AFL) and later the National Football League (NFL).
He later served as the executive director of the National Football League Players' Association (NFLPA).
In 1987, he was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame.
He is also the only player in NFL history to reach the Super Bowl in three different decades with the same team.
Herbert Mark Thomas (May 26, 1902 in Sampson City, Florida – December 4, 1991 in Starke, Florida) was a right-handed minor and major league baseball player and minor league manager.
Although his major league career lasted only three seasons, 1924, 1925 and 1927, for two different teams, the Boston Braves and New York Giants, his minor league career spanned 17 seasons.
He played professionally in three different decades.

British band Duran Duran have released fourteen studio albums, three remix albums, two live albums, four compilation albums, two box sets, five EPs, thirty-nine singles, fourteen video albums on various record labels. Duran Duran have sold over 100 million records.

The band is one of the few artists to achieve US top 10 albums in three different decades (1980s, 1990s and 2010s) and one of even fewer to reach the top 20 in all four decades of their existence: five albums in the 1980s ("Duran Duran", "Rio", "Seven and the Ragged Tiger", "Arena" and "Notorious"), three albums in the 1990s ("Liberty", "Duran Duran" [aka "The Wedding Album"] and "Thank You"), one album in the 2000s ("Astronaut") and one album in the 2010s ("Paper Gods").

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

The `summary` must be a concise (typically 1–4 short sentences or clauses), self-contained intermediate state that helps the next agent locate the missing second-hop evidence. Do not try to produce the final answer. Follow these rules when composing the `summary`:

1) First-hop evidence: In one short clause, paraphrase or quote (<=15 words) the specific fact from the passages that is relevant to the question and that you are using as the bridge. Include the passage title or identifier that contains this evidence.

2) Bridge / missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity or the specific missing fact that must be resolved in the next hop (for example: an ambiguous given name that must be identified, the named person whose birthplace is required, the tournament title whose venues must be counted, or which film credit remains unverified). If the passages already give a full name, use that full name as the bridge entity; if only a given name or ambiguous identifier is present, state that the identity is unresolved.

3) Retrieval direction: Provide one concise, actionable next-hop retrieval plan (1–3 ordered steps or 1–2 short search-query templates). Use clear placeholders where appropriate (e.g., "fetch the biography page for <NAME>", "search \"<NAME> birthplace\" site:wikipedia.org", "open the '<TOURNAMENT>' page and inspect its Venues/Matches/Schedule section", or "fetch '<FILM>' cast list and compare to '<OTHER FILM>' cast"). Specify which page/title or which page sections the next agent should inspect. Do not perform retrieval or assert any facts that are not present in the given passages.

4) Disambiguation: If the question's referent is ambiguous (e.g., only a given name is provided), request the user's clarification in one short sentence only (e.g., "If you mean a different Maurice, please provide the full name."). Do not ask permission to search; just state the needed clarification.

5) Constraints and tone: Never invent or assert unverified facts (no guessed winners, years, birthplaces, counts, or cast overlaps). Do not output the final answer. Keep language neutral, concise, and actionable. Avoid long verbatim extracts beyond a short supporting quote.

Output exactly one field: `summary` (a single string following the above structure).
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `summary_1`, produce the field `query`.

Output requirements
- Return exactly one compact, single-line retrieval query string in the field `query`. Do not return any other fields, commentary, punctuation, quotes, or the answer.
- Keep the query short and retrieval-oriented (keyword bag, typically ~3–7 meaningful tokens). Prefer tokens already present in `question` or `summary_1`.

Rules for composing the query
1) Nonprofit medical-practice/facility questions: If the question asks about a non-profit/nonprofit medical practice or facility (or uses similar wording) and `summary_1` contains an organization name, do NOT repeat that organization name. Instead produce a concise bridge query combining the place token from the question, the state/region token, and a class term such as "nonprofit medical practice" or "clinic". Use the template: <place> <state> nonprofit medical practice clinic

2) Avoid hypothesizing names: If the needed facility or organization name is not supported by `question` or `summary_1`, do not invent or assert candidate names. Reuse the topic and the expressed information need (topic + class + location) to form the query.

3) Comparative person-role questions: If the question compares two people (e.g., which person fills a larger variety of roles) and the inputs name both persons or `summary_1` lists one person's roles, produce one compact query that names both people and includes concise role/occupation keywords (examples: director producer composer musician writer actor). You may include a brief source token such as Wikipedia to guide retrieval, but remain compact. Do not ask for the final comparison or return conclusions.

General prohibitions
- Do not output full sentences, long explanatory text, lists of candidate organizations, or claims/answers.
- Do not add metadata, instructions, or extra fields—only the single query string.

Produce the `query` field now according to the rules above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, and `passages`, produce the field `summary` (string). Preserve the native input/output interface exactly and return only the `summary` field. Do not produce any other fields, headers, or meta-commentary.

Task: Integrate the newly retrieved `passages` with the existing `context` so the result is a grounded, post-retrieval state that the final-answer agent can use. Keep the summary concise, factual, and limited to information present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`.

Required behavior (apply the relevant rules to each input):
- Preserve facts already asserted in `context` unless the `passages` contradict them. If passages contradict a contextual claim, state the contradiction briefly and retain uncertainty rather than inventing a resolution.
- When a passage (or passages) explicitly supports a claim found in `context`, do all of the following in the `summary`:
  1) Briefly restate the claim from `context` in one short clause.
  2) Identify which retrieved passage(s) provide explicit support by naming the passage title (or a short identifier) in parentheses and give a one-line paraphrase or at most one short quoted fragment drawn directly from that passage as corroboration. Keep paraphrases short and avoid long verbatim spans.
  3) If other passage(s) do not state the claim but supply useful metadata (for example: title, year, director, occupation, full name/alias, location details), list those retained metadata facts in one short clause per passage.
  4) Finish with one concise integration sentence that synthesizes the evidence: state which passage explicitly supports the claim and which passage(s) only supply metadata for downstream use. Resolve the earlier bridge (i.e., commit to the claim) only if explicit support is present; otherwise keep the summary phrased to reflect remaining uncertainty.
- If a passage links two entities (e.g., a facility name to an address or locality) and that link resolves a location or relationship asserted in `context`, include a single short paraphrase that records that linking fact and then a brief integrated sentence that ties that link back to the studio/person/film in question.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Output must be concise (about 1–4 sentences), factual, and limited to content present in the inputs. Prefer 1–3 sentences when the integration is simple.
- Do not add new facts, speculation, or external knowledge beyond `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
- Avoid long quotations; at most one short quoted fragment per supporting passage and only when it directly helps attribute support.
- Do not use hedging language when the passages state facts plainly. Use hedging only when the inputs are ambiguous or contradictory.
- Do not include dataset labels, internal notes, evaluation, or extraneous formatting. Do not produce the final answer outside this `summary` field.

Goal: deliver a compact, evidence-attributed summary that (a) restates the first-hop claim when present, (b) explicitly attributes which passage(s) support that claim and which provide metadata, and (c) synthesizes the evidence so the downstream final-answer agent can use it to answer the question.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional conservative, evidence-driven normalizations and tie-breaking rules (small, shared improvements):
- Use summary_2 as the later integrated evidence and give it priority when summaries disagree about the answer, but do not ignore an explicit short named-entity asserted clearly in summary_1: if either summary contains a concise explicit attribution (for example, phrasing like "directed by <NAME>", "headquartered in <PLACE>", or a direct statement that names the immediate jurisdiction), accept that explicit short named-entity as the supported minimal answer unless the other summary gives a different explicit short named-entity (in that conflicting-explicit case, emit a brief clarification question). In short: prefer explicit concise assertions wherever present; otherwise prefer summary_2 when resolving conflicts.
- When the two summaries agree exactly on a short named phrase that includes a trailing parenthetical disambiguator or trailing parenthetical abbreviation (final ' ( ... )'), strip that single final parenthetical and emit the pre-parenthesis core name (trim surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. Similarly, when the identical agreed phrase ends with the exact suffix ", United States", remove that trailing ", United States" and emit the remaining shorter place string.
- More generally, when an agreed short answer contains trailing clarifying material (a final parentheses block, a trailing parenthetical abbreviation, or an appositive beginning with a comma) and the core name up to the first '(' or the first comma appears verbatim in both summaries, emit that core substring rather than the longer qualified form.
- If the question explicitly requests a size (phrases like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size') and both summaries are identical and contain a phrase of the form '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less', emit only the '<size>' portion (number plus unit) verbatim as the minimal answer (remove the trailing inclusive qualifier).
- If the chosen minimal answer candidate is a contiguous digits-only token sequence (regex ^\d+$), convert it to its spelled-out English words (lowercase, with conventional hyphenation) and emit that word-form as the answer.
- For 'what do X and Y have in common' style questions where both summaries contain the same plural noun token as the shared attribute, emit the singular form of that noun (apply simple regular singularization rules such as removing a trailing 's' or converting 'ies' -> 'y').
- Always prefer a concise explicit short named-entity present verbatim in either summary over asking for clarification when the other summary merely reports inability or uncertainty; however, if the summaries provide two different explicit names for the same field, emit a concise clarification question rather than guessing.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: For Which team did the second man to win a championship in three different decades play ?
hop1_docs: Indian cricket team in Zimbabwe in 2005 | The Indian cricket team toured Zimbabwe for cricket matches in August and September 2005.  The Indians played in the Videocon Tri-Series in Zimbabwe, winning three of five matches but losing the final to finish second in the three-team tournament, and they played Zimbabwe for two Test matches in September 2005.  The hosts Zimbabwe were ninth out of ten teams in the ICC Test Championship, with their last win against the top eight Test nations coming in June 2001, against India.  They did not manage to win a match here either, as India, led by the performances of fast bowler Irfan Pathan (who took 21 wickets), won the series 2–0 after an innings win in the first Test and a 10-wicket win in the second.  India consolidated their third place in the Test championship with the win, which was also their first series win in Zimbabwe in four attempts.

John Salley | John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964) is an American retired professional basketball player, actor and talk show host.  He was the first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises, as well as the first player (and only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan) in the NBA to win a championship in three different decades.

2008 Dutch Caribbean Age Group Championships | 2008 Dutch Caribbean Age Group Championships is a championship for young Track & Field athletes of the islands of the Dutch Kingdom.  In 2008 there were three island competing.  Aruba, Curaçao and Sint Maarten.  They compete in different events and the athlete with the overall most points will be declared the winner.  There are two age groups, they are 10–11 years (Pentathlon) and 12–13 years (Heptathlon).  This year was the first year that hurdles and baseball throw was added to the event list for the heptathlon.  Sint Eustatius did not have a complete team.  They only competed with a full Heptathlon team.  Like every year, the hosting island is allowed to have two teams (which was Aruba).  There were 32 athletes competing in total and Curaçao was declared the overall winner, Aruba came in second and Sint Maarten came in third.  This would also mark the 4th win for Curaçao

1894–95 British Home Championship | The 1894-95 British Home Championship was an international football tournament played between the British Home Nations.  The competition was won by England, who like second placed Wales, did not lose a game.  Wales however failed to win one either, scoring three draws and so finishing behind England.  Scotland took joint second place with three points gained from a win, draw and a loss.  Ireland came last with a single point garnered from their draw with Wales.

1920–21 British Home Championship | The 1920-21 British Home Championship was a football tournament played between the British Home Nations during the 1920–21 season.  The second tournament played since the hiatus of the First World War, the 1921 competition was dominated by Scotland, who won the first of seven championships they would claim throughout the decade.  England and reigning champions Wales came joint second as goal difference was not at this stage used to separate teams.

1978–79 British Home Championship | The 1978-79 British Home Championship was a British Home Nations competition, won by the English football side and notable for seeing marked increases in hooliganism and falling attendance which would result in its cancellation in 1984.  The English started well, beating Northern Ireland to match the heavy Welsh victory over Scotland on the same day, which featured a hat trick by John Toshack.  Scotland recovered by beating the Irish in their next match while England and Wales played out a goalless draw, leaving three sides theoretically capable of winning the Championship in the final round.  Wales could only manage a draw with the Irish and so in the deciding match between England and Scotland, a 1–1 half time score gave the Scots some hope but a strong second half performance from England was rewarded with a deserved 3–1 win.  This result gave England the Championship, with Wales in second place.  The tournament also saw the introduction of goal difference to separate teams, although it had no effect on the eventual outcome.

1955–56 British Home Championship | The 1955-56 British Home Championship was a football tournament played during the 1955–56 season between the British Home Nations.  It was the only occasion during the hundred-year run of the Home Championship in which all four teams finished level on points.  As goal difference was not used to determine position until 1979, all four teams shared the trophy, holding it for three months each.  Had goal difference been used to determine the winner, then England would have won with Scotland second.
hop1_titles: Indian cricket team in Zimbabwe in 2005
response.summary_1: John Salley [2]: quotes "the other being Tim Duncan" as also winning championships in three decades; Bridge: Tim Duncan — which NBA team/franchise did he play for?; Next: open the "Tim Duncan" Wikipedia/biography page and inspect the lead/infobox and "Playing career" (or search "Tim Duncan team site:wikipedia.org") to read the team name.
hop2_docs: Tim Duncan (American football) | Tim Duncan (born June 12, 1979) is a former American football placekicker who played in the National Football League (NFL) for the Arizona Cardinals.  He played college football at the University of Oklahoma.  Duncan also played for Kentucky State University in the division II SIAC conference during the 1997 season when he helped the Thoroughbreds win the inaugural Pioneer Bowl.  He transferred to Oklahoma the following season.

Tricia Duncan | Patricia "Tricia" Duncan (born August 21, 1973) is a retired swimmer for the U.S. Virgin Islands who participated in the backstroke at the 1988 Summer Olympics.  Duncan finished 34th in the 100 m backstroke and 30th in 200 m backstroke.  She is the older sister of NBA basketball player Tim Duncan.  Duncan attended Swarthmore College.

Ray Duncan | Raymond Twomey "Ray" Duncan (October 23, 1930 – October 9, 2015) was an American entrepreneur and vintner.  Originally involved in oil entrepreneurship in Colorado and founder of Duncan Oil, he was the founder along with Justin Meyer of Silver Oak Cellars in 1972, a successful winery based in the Napa Valley and Alexander Valley.  Today his sons David Duncan and Tim Duncan run Silver Oak Cellars, as well as Twomey Cellars.  Duncan established the Durango Mountain "Purgatory" Ski Resort in 1965 and was chairman of the board for the ski industry organization Colorado Ski Country USA.  He was inducted into the Colorado Ski and Snowboard Hall of Fame in 2006.

IBM Award | The IBM Award was an award given out to National Basketball Association players from 1984 to 2002.  The award was sponsored and calculated by technology company IBM and was determined by a computer formula, which measured a player's statistical contribution to his team.  The player with the best contribution to his team in the league received the award.  The first recipient was Magic Johnson of the Los Angeles Lakers, and the final recipient was Tim Duncan of the San Antonio Spurs.

DBpedia | DBpedia (from "DB" for "database") is a project aiming to extract structured content from the information created in the Wikipedia project.  This structured information is made available on the World Wide Web.  DBpedia allows users to semantically query relationships and properties of Wikipedia resources, including links to other related datasets. Tim Berners-Lee described DBpedia as one of the most famous parts of the decentralized Linked Data effort.

David Duncan (vintner) | David Duncan (born 1965) is an American vintner and entrepreneur.  The President and Chief Executive Officer of Duncan Oil, he is also the President and Chief Executive Officer of Silver Oak Cellars and Managing Partner of Twomey Cellars, successful wineries in northern California, operating in the Napa, Alexander and Russian River Valleys.  He is the son of late Ray Duncan, an oil and wine entrepreneur who co-founded Silver Oak in 1972 with Justin Meyer.  Silver Oak is a family-run and owned business, and his brother Tim Duncan is Executive Vice President of the company. " ColoradoBiz" says that David Duncan is "as much a hands-on winery president as he can be", as president of a winery that has "reached cult status" for its Cabernet Sauvignon production.

Silver Oak Cellars | Silver Oak Cellars is a family-owned, California winery dedicated to producing only Cabernet Sauvignon.  Silver Oak was established in 1972 by Ray Duncan and Justin Meyer.  It is currently operated by Ray's sons David Duncan, who serves as the President and CEO, and his brother, Tim Duncan, who serves as Executive Vice President.  Silver Oak has two wineries; one in Oakville in the Napa Valley and one in Geyserville in the Alexander Valley.  The Duncan Family also established Twomey Cellars in 1999, named after Ray Duncan’s mother, where they produce Pinot noir, Merlot and Sauvignon blanc.  Twomey has wineries in Calistoga and Healdsburg.
response.summary_2: Context claim: Tim Duncan is the other player John Salley named as winning championships in three different decades. The retrieved passages do not explicitly confirm the three‑decade championship claim, but Passage [4] (IBM Award) identifies him with the San Antonio Spurs — it states he was the final recipient, “Tim Duncan of the San Antonio Spurs.” Passage [2] (Tricia Duncan) supplies metadata that Tim is an NBA player and her brother, and Passage [1] refers to a different Tim Duncan (American football). Integration: although the passages don’t explicitly verify the three‑decade championships, they identify Tim Duncan as a San Antonio Spurs player (Passage [4]) with supporting metadata from Passage [2] and a disambiguation in Passage [1].
